text: The surgeon clears tissue around the hernia defect, revealing a previously placed mesh with good tissue integration. The hernia sac is excised to prevent post-operative seromas, followed by excision of peritoneum around the defect. Using a robotic platform, the surgeon closes the defect with a two-zero polypropylene barbed suture in a continuous fashion, ensuring a snug closure without tissue strangulation.

The surgeon performs a second row of suturing to ensure a snug repair, considering the patient's history of hernia recurrence. The adhesiolysis is completed, and the mesh is placed and begins fixation using trans-fascial sutures at the twelve o'clock and six o'clock positions. The surgeon uses three ports, including one for the camera and two for robotic instruments, to ensure the mesh lies flat for subsequent suturing. The initial fixation point is at the near side, either three o'clock or nine o'clock, followed by the far end fixation.

The surgeon uses a polypropylene barbed suture to perform non-strangulating fixation in the right superior quadrant of the surgical field. The suturing technique involves taking interrupted bites with a Vicryl 3-0 suture, ensuring good apposition without causing pain. The surgeon holds the needle with a needle driver in the right hand and uses a pro grasp instrument in the left hand, while the assistant cuts the suture to minimize downtime. The mesh being used is a 15x15 cm Ventralite dual-layered mesh.

The surgeon begins suturing the left side of the mesh on the patient's left side, ensuring the ports are positioned 8 to 10 centimeters away from the mesh edge to facilitate suturing. Care is taken to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels during suturing in the inferior quadrant to prevent bleeding or hematoma. The surgeon uses a technique to insert multiple switches at the start of the procedure to reduce downtime.
